preFix.title: Paper clay geometric wall tiles set
preFix.subtitle: Nine incised square tiles arranged as a 3 by 3 grid
preFix.excerpt: A set of nine 80 mm square paper clay tiles, each with a different incised geometric pattern, arranged as a 3 by 3 grid wall display. No kiln needed. PVA sealed and foam-pad mounted.

content.text: This set of nine 80 mm square tiles is made from
paper clay

and displayed as a 3 by 3 grid on a wall. Each tile carries a different incised geometric pattern: diagonal lines, chevrons, a grid, concentric squares, triangles, hexagonal honeycomb, diamonds, a dot field, and a blank plain tile as the centre. Paper clay is used here because the cut corners and incised lines of geometric designs in plain air-dry
greenware
are crack-prone at drying; the cellulose fibre content reduces this significantly. No kiln is required. Tiles are PVA sealed and mounted with double-sided foam pads.
Preparation

Sketch each of the nine geometric patterns on paper at 80 mm by 80 mm scale, marking the spacing of lines. This planning step prevents improvising on the clay and ensures the repeat units fit the tile consistently. Cut 6 mm spacer guides from card. Cut a 80 mm by 80 mm square card template for consistent cutting. Note which pattern goes in which grid position before rolling any clay: having a plan for the 3 by 3 arrangement means you can balance bold and fine-lined tiles across the grid rather than ending up with all the complex patterns clustered in one area.
Construction
For each tile: wedge approximately 65 g of paper clay and flatten on the wooden board. Place 6 mm spacer cards on either side and roll to an even 6 mm thickness.
Place the 80 mm square card template on the clay and cut around it with the fettling knife pressed against a ruler. Lift the off-cuts away. Smooth the top surface lightly with a barely damp sponge.
Leave each cut tile to firm to
leather-hard
before incising. Roll all nine tiles first, then go back to the first tile and begin incising : by the time you have rolled the last tile, the first should be firm enough to carve cleanly.

Incise each tile pattern from the sketches. Use the needle tool for fine lines and the sgraffito tool for wider, flatter grooves. Draw lines in single continuous passes rather than repeatedly scoring the same line. Guide the needle against a ruler for straight geometric lines. The nine patterns are: (1) diagonal parallel lines spaced 10 mm apart, (2) chevrons in rows, (3) a 4 by 4 ruled grid, (4) concentric squares at 10 mm intervals, (5) a triangular grid, (6) hexagonal honeycomb cells approximately 15 mm across, (7) diamond grid, (8) a 5 by 5 dot matrix made by pressing the blunt needle end, (9) blank plain tile with a single border line only.
Once each tile is incised, smooth any raised clay burr along the groove edges with a barely damp fingertip pressed lightly along the line. Do not wet the surface enough to soften the clay.

Set all nine tiles flat on the wooden board to dry. Label the back of each tile lightly in pencil with its pattern name and grid position (A1 to C3) while the clay is leather-hard, for reference during mounting.
Drying
Dry all nine tiles on a flat wooden board for 36 to 2 days until
bone-dry

. At 6 mm thick, these tiles dry faster than 8 mm pieces and are correspondingly more brittle at bone-dry. Paper clay reduces corner cracking compared with plain air-dry clay, but handle all nine tiles with care once bone-dry: pick them up from the flat face rather than the edges. If any tile warps on drying, do not force it flat at bone-dry. Minor warps will be concealed by the foam-pad mounting method.
Finishing

Apply two coats of undiluted PVA to all surfaces of each tile. Work the brush into the incised grooves on the front face. Leave each coat to dry clear. Once sealed, lay the tiles face-down in their 3 by 3 grid arrangement and apply two double-sided foam pads to the reverse of each tile : one pad near each of two opposite corners. The foam pads hold the tile to the wall and absorb any minor warp. Press each tile firmly onto the wall in position and hold for 30 seconds. A 10 mm gap between each tile in the 3 by 3 arrangement gives the grid a clear separation.
Care

Dust with a dry soft-bristled brush. Do not use damp cloths on the incised surface : water entering the grooves over time can loosen the PVA seal. If a tile is knocked off the wall, re-attach with fresh foam pads: the adhesive on foam pads degrades over two to three years. Re-seal with a thin PVA coat if the surface looks powdery.
Variations
Colour accent: before sealing, rub diluted acrylic paint into the incised grooves and wipe the flat surface clean. The pattern reads in colour against the natural white clay.
Two-tone grid: paint alternating tiles with a dilute grey wash and leave the rest natural white. This creates a checkerboard colour pattern across the geometric grid.
Make the set as 12 tiles in a 3 by 4 arrangement for a larger wall installation, adding three more patterns: a wave, a scallop, and a brick-bond line.
Use a single pattern across all nine tiles for a uniform textured wall panel effect rather than a sampler grid.

rows.fix: Wait until the clay surface does not stick to a fingertip before carving. At leather-hard, a line can be drawn without the clay tearing or the groove walls crumbling.
rows.cause: Clay too soft at carving : not yet leather-hard
rows.problem: Incised lines are jagged with torn edges

rows.fix: Hold the ruler firmly against the clay surface and draw the line toward yourself in one slow pull. Let vertical lines dry for 30 seconds before crossing with horizontal lines.
rows.cause: Not using a ruler, or ruler slipped mid-stroke
rows.problem: Lines in the geometric grid do not meet at corners cleanly

rows.fix: Always handle bone-dry tiles from the flat face. Store face-up on a flat surface. Small corner chips can be repaired by pressing a small amount of paper clay slip into the break, drying, and sanding smooth.
rows.cause: Thin cut corner, or tile picked up by the edge
rows.problem: Corner of tile chips off during handling at bone-dry

rows.fix: Use a ruler and pencil to mark the wall positions lightly in pencil before adhering. Mark the centre of each tile position and align the foam-padded tile back to it.
rows.cause: Foam pads placed at different heights, or tiles measured informally
rows.problem: Grid tiles do not align evenly on the wall

rows.fix: Apply PVA with a liner brush inside grooves and then immediately draw a dry mop brush lightly over the surface to wick out excess. The groove should remain visible but sealed.
rows.cause: Too much PVA pooling in the grooves
rows.problem: PVA fills the incised grooves and the pattern disappears
